title: How Much Would You Pay to Have the Most Vivid TV Display on the Block?
gist: A hands-on review of LG's $5,000 Micro RGB Evo TV places it at the premium top of a crowded new RGB LED TV category, arguing it matches OLED only after heavy tinkering and costs roughly double the Hisense UR9 for far less than double the quality.

themes.theme: Micro vs mini RGB LED category emerges

insights.claim: Micro and mini RGB LED TVs, which emit tiny red, green, and blue lights, are being positioned as the newest display innovation offering better color accuracy, contrast, brightness, and blacks than traditional LED and OLED sets.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Signals a new premium TV upgrade cycle that could pressure OLED positioning at the high end.
insights.quote: The new LG Micro RGB Evo might well make you question your OLED purchase from a few years ago.

insights.claim: Mini RGB uses slightly larger LEDs than micro RGB, theoretically affording less display control, but the reviewer judges whether the two yield a notably different visual effect to be genuinely up for debate.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: The micro/mini distinction may be more marketing tiering than perceptible value, informing where the real price/performance line sits.
insights.quote: But whether the LED cousins yield a notably different visual effect is up for debate.
insights.locator: The two terms are not interchangeable

insights.claim: The RGB TV field spans wide price points: LG Micro RGB Evo starts at $5,000 (75-inch), Samsung Micro RGB R95H at $3,000 (65-inch), TCL RM9L at $6,000 (85-inch), Hisense UR9 at $2,200 (65-inch), and Sony Bravia 7 Mark II at $2,600 (65-inch).
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Maps competitive pricing across the new category; Hisense and Sony undercut LG by roughly half.
insights.locator: The RGB TV market is quite crowded

insights.claim: The reviewer concludes the LG Micro RGB Evo costs twice as much as the Hisense 65-inch UR9 but does not deliver double the quality, and for vibrancy alone a buyer may be just as well served by the cheaper Hisense.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Value shoppers may migrate to Hisense/Sony tiers, squeezing ultra-premium TV margins.
insights.quote: It also costs twice as much as the Hisense 65-inch UR9, but I don't think you'll get double the quality.
insights.locator: Ultimately, the LG Micro RGB Evo is on par

insights.claim: LG continues to use its own WebOS (now version 26) rather than switching to Google TV as recent Sony, Hisense, and TCL models have done.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Google TV is consolidating the smart-TV OS layer while LG holds out, relevant to platform/ad-revenue battles.
insights.quote: Unlike recent Sony, Hisense, and TCL models, LG hasn't switched to Google TV; instead, it uses its own WebOS, now in version 26.
insights.locator: Unlike recent Sony, Hisense, and TCL models

insights.claim: LG's premium TV monetizes recurring add-ons, charging $5 per month via LG Gallery+ for access to about 4,500 curated artworks for its screensaver art mode.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: Illustrates how TV makers layer subscription revenue onto hardware.
insights.locator: I also tested the screensaver mode

insights.claim: The LG set ships with dated connectivity, using Wi-Fi 5 while the cheaper Hisense UR9 uses the more advanced Wi-Fi 6E.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: A premium-priced product lagging on a spec its cheaper rival leads on undercuts the value case.

insights.claim: The LG Micro RGB Evo matches OLED only after heavy manual tuning and still trails OLED on contrast, notably in rendering black trees against a dark mountain.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: OLED retains a contrast edge, limiting how much the RGB LED category can displace it out of the box.
insights.quote: OLED still has the upper hand on contrast, more crisply displaying black trees in the foreground of a dark mountain.

insights.claim: The TV's advertised 330 Hz VRR mode (LG's Motion Booster) produced flickering and rainbow artifacts in every PC game tested, forcing a fallback to 165 Hz, an issue LG says it is investigating.
insights.speaker: John Brandon
insights.so_what: A flagship shipping with broken headline gaming specs signals QA gaps at premium price points.
insights.quote: But with that mode enabled, I kept seeing weird flickering and rainbow effects in Crimson Desert and every other PC game I tried.
